title: Betting, Kelly & Risk of Ruin
description: Having edge isn't enough — size it right or the market takes everything back.

levels.title: Edge & Odds
intro: Bookmaker odds encode probabilities: odds of 3:1 against mean implied probability 1/(3+1) = 25%. Your edge is your probability estimate minus the implied one.
Positive EV per bet (Unit 2) is the entry ticket. This unit is about what EV alone doesn't tell you: how much to bet.

exercises.prompt: A coin lands heads 60% of the time. You bet $1 at even money on heads. EV per bet in dollars?
exercises.explanation: 0.6 × (+1) + 0.4 × (−1) = +$0.20. A 60/40 coin at even odds is a 20% edge — enormous by real-market standards.

exercises.prompt: Betting odds of 3:1 against an outcome imply what probability of it happening?
exercises.explanation: 3:1 against = risk 1 to win 3 = break-even probability 1/(1+3) = 25%. If YOUR estimate is above 25%, the bet has positive EV.

exercises.prompt: A bookmaker's implied probabilities across all outcomes sum to 1.05. What is their margin (the 'vig'), in percent?
exercises.explanation: Fair probabilities sum to exactly 1. The extra 0.05 = 5% is the bookmaker's built-in edge — their version of the bid-ask spread from Unit 4.

exercises.prompt: A bet has positive EV. What ELSE must you consider before betting big?
options: Nothing — positive EV means bet max
Risk of ruin: a bad streak can wipe your bankroll before the edge pays off
exercises.explanation: EV is a long-run average — you only reach the long run if you survive the short run. Bet too large and variance kills you before expectation saves you.

exercises.prompt: You win each $1 even-money bet with probability 0.55. Expected profit over 100 bets, in dollars?
exercises.explanation: EV per bet = 0.55 − 0.45 = $0.10; times 100 = $10. But the SD over 100 bets is ≈ $10 too — the edge and the noise are the same size. Hence: sizing.

levels.title: The Kelly Criterion
intro: Kelly answers 'what fraction f of my bankroll should I bet?' to maximize long-run growth: f* = (bp − q)/b, where b = payout odds, p = win prob, q = 1−p.
For even-money bets it simplifies to f* = 2p − 1 = your edge. Overbetting is punished brutally: at 2× Kelly your long-run growth drops to zero.

exercises.prompt: For an even-money bet with win probability p, the Kelly fraction is:
exercises.explanation: With b = 1: f* = (p − q)/1 = p − (1−p) = 2p − 1. A 55% coin → bet 10% of bankroll; a 50% coin → bet nothing (no edge, no bet).

exercises.prompt: A 60% coin at even money. What fraction of your bankroll does Kelly say to bet? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: f* = 2(0.6) − 1 = 0.2, i.e. 20%. Feels aggressive? It is — full Kelly still suffers ~50% drawdowns. Most pros bet a fraction of it.

exercises.prompt: A bet pays 2:1 (win $2 per $1 staked) and you win with probability 0.4. Kelly fraction f* = (bp − q)/b? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: (2×0.4 − 0.6)/2 = (0.8 − 0.6)/2 = 0.1 → bet 10%. Note the bet has +EV (0.4×2 − 0.6 = +0.2) but Kelly still says only 10% — growth optimization ≠ EV maximization.

exercises.prompt: You consistently bet exactly DOUBLE the Kelly fraction. In the long run your bankroll growth rate is:
exercises.explanation: Growth as a function of bet size is a hill with its peak at Kelly; 2× Kelly sits where growth crosses zero. Beyond that you're compounding toward ruin — WITH a positive-EV bet. Volatility drag is real.

exercises.prompt: Why do most practitioners bet HALF Kelly?
options: It gives ~75% of the growth with ~half the variance, and protects against overestimating your edge
exercises.explanation: The growth curve is flat near the top: half Kelly sacrifices little growth but slashes drawdowns. And since your 'p' is an estimate, betting less hedges the biggest risk — being wrong about your own edge.

levels.title: Ruin & Survival
intro: Gambler's ruin: betting $1 on a FAIR coin, starting with $a and stopping at $N or $0, your chance of reaching $N is exactly a/N. With any house edge it's far worse.
Compounding is multiplicative: −50% then +50% is NOT breakeven. Surviving drawdowns beats maximizing headlines.

exercises.prompt: Fair-coin gambler's ruin: you start with $20, bet $1 at a time, and stop at $100 or $0. Probability you reach $100? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: For a fair game it's start/target = 20/100 = 0.2. Elegant proof: your bankroll is a martingale, so E[final] = $20 = P(win)×100, giving P = 0.2.

exercises.prompt: The Martingale strategy (double your bet after every loss) 'guarantees' a profit. What's the flaw?
options: A long losing streak hits your bankroll/table limit, and that rare huge loss exactly offsets all the small wins
It works — that's why casinos lose money
exercises.explanation: After n losses you must stake 2ⁿ. Ten straight losses on a near-50/50 bet (~1 in 1000) demands 1024 units. EV was never positive; Martingale just hides the risk in a fat left tail.

exercises.prompt: Your $100 portfolio loses 50%, then gains 50%. Final value in dollars?
exercises.explanation: 100 → 50 → 75. Percentage gains and losses don't cancel: a −50% needs +100% to recover. Multiplicative processes punish volatility.

exercises.prompt: That gap between the average return (0%) and what you actually ended with (−25%) is called:
options: Volatility drag — geometric mean < arithmetic mean
exercises.explanation: Geometric growth ≈ arithmetic mean − σ²/2. Volatility itself eats compound returns — the deep reason Kelly punishes oversized bets and why 2× Kelly grows at zero.

exercises.prompt: A strategy returns 8% annually with 12% volatility; the risk-free rate is 2%. Sharpe ratio? (decimal)
exercises.explanation: (8 − 2)/12 = 0.5 excess return per unit of risk. Sharpe puts every strategy on one scale — a 40% return at 80% vol is WORSE than 8% at 12%.
